brief: First registered user (userId=0) cannot access any protected resources. All other users work fine.

root_cause.description: The authentication middleware uses a JavaScript falsy check `if (!userId)` to guard protected routes. For the first registered user whose userId is the integer 0, `!0` evaluates to true, causing the middleware to reject a valid authenticated user as if no userId were present.
root_cause.mechanism: JavaScript falsy coercion: `!0` evaluates to true, causing valid userId=0 to fail the authentication guard

required_findings.description: Agent must identify the authentication middleware as the failing subsystem
Agent must identify the JavaScript falsy coercion on the userId value as the mechanism
Agent must specifically identify userId=0 (the first user) as the affected value
Agent must identify auth.ts line 28 as the exact location of the falsy guard

anti_patterns.description: The JWT is generated and signed correctly; the defect is in how the middleware interprets the extracted userId value
The user record exists and is valid; the middleware rejects the userId before any database lookup occurs
Session and cookie handling are not involved; the bug is in the numeric type check applied to the decoded userId

notes: The fix is to replace `if (!userId)` with `if (userId === undefined || userId === null)` or equivalently `if (userId == null)`. A score-4 investigation will note that string-encoded user IDs (e.g., UUIDs) are immune to this bug because an empty string '' is the falsy edge case for strings, not '0'. Integer primary keys starting at 0 are the specific risk.
